title: Commit ('C')
text: Commit ('C') Commit the transaction on the current logon path. If the DBMS does not support Commit, return 0 to Uniface. Description Modes: Mode 0—perform a normal commit. Mode 1—perform the first phase (prepare for commit) of the two-phase commit protocol on the current logon path. Mode 2—perform the second phase (commit) of the two-phase commit protocol on the current logon path. Note: In some DBMSs, the commit connector request closes all open cursors. To see if this is the case for your DBMS, refer to your DBMS documentation. From Uniface Preconditions realized by Uniface—the connector is logged on to the DBMS. Table 1. Information Available from Uniface Data Structure->Element Description uctrl->iobuf Points to the general I/O buffer. uctrl->sqlbuf Points to the SQL buffer. uctrl->uchfw Uniface virtual address of the next control block. uctrl->uchbw Uniface virtual address of the previous control block. uctrl->udrvini Unique identification of the current logon path. uctrl->udrvini Unique identification of the current logon path. uctrl->udrvnum Connector sequence number. uctrl->ulock Lock indicator. To Uniface If the connector maintains the field uctrl->ulock and the Commit succeeded (or the Commit failed and the DBMS implicitly performed a Rollback), uctrl->ulock must be set to 0 for every control block that meets all the following criteria. It is for the DBMS driven by this connector ( uctrl->udrvnum equals udrvnum in the first control block). It belongs to the current logon path ( uctrl->udrvini equals udrvini in the first control block). The corresponding table is open ( uctrl->uopen is greater than 0). Table 2. Connector Request Return Values Value Description 0 Commit was successful. -3 Exceptional error. -9 I/O server logon error. -16 DBMS reported network error. If the connector request return value is less than 0, an error code must be supplied in uctrl->udrverr . Note: In some DBMSs the Rollback connector request closes all open cursors. To see if this is the case for your DBMS, refer to your DBMS documentation. Extra information Uniface has no built-in knowledge of the transaction concept because this is the responsibility of the application developer. A Commit is only requested when the ProcScript command commit is executed, and a Rollback is only requested when the ProcScript command rollback is executed. The conventional meaning of Commit is to signal the successful completion of a transaction to the DBMS. The DBMS then makes all pending changes on the current logon path in the database permanent, and releases all locks. The conventional meaning of Rollback is to signal unsuccessful completion of a transaction to the DBMS. The DBMS then undoes all pending changes on the current logon path and releases all locks. Uniface does not start a new transaction explicitly. If the DBMS requires an explicit start of transaction, the connector must provide it. The following strategies are commonly used: Start a transaction immediately after a Logon, Rollback and Commit. Start a transaction as soon as it is required, for example when the first Update, Write is requested. The Commit mode 1 and mode 2 can only be requested if uinfo->twophase was set to three in the Info call. A Commit mode 1 and Commit mode 2 can be followed by a Commit mode 0. When using the two-phase commit protocol, Uniface does not behave as ‘coordinator’ or ‘resource manager’. The Commit and Rollback have the first control block in a list of available control blocks. The first control block is a dummy block that is used to exchange data between Uniface and the Commit or Rollback request. The first control block has no associated connector workspace ( uctrl->udrv cannot be used) and it is not open ( uctrl->uopen is 0). Restore the original control block before returning to Uniface. TM Controlled Connectors Connectors that are TM-controlled ( uctrl->TMEnabled=1 ) do not receive any Commit or Rollback requests. The transaction manager sends these requests directly to the DBMS. Therefore, TM-controlled DBMS connectors cannot perform resource management and internal administration when a transaction has ended. Also, TM-controlled DBMS connectors cannot administer uctrl->ulock . Related Links uctrl Exchanges general information between Uniface and the connector, and describes a table. TM-Controlled DBMS Connectors A database connector can be controlled by a transaction manager only if it is TM-capable. Uniface does not provide TM-controlled connectors and does not directly support third-party transaction managers. However, you can use the APIs provided by the Database Connector Interface to build a custom database connector that does. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
